coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen absorption–desorption characteristics of Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) (0≤ X ≤0.12) alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Cho, Sung-Wook
Park, Choong-Nyeon
Yoo, Jeong-Hyun
Choi, Jeon
Park, Je-Shin
Suh, Chang-Yul
Shim, Gunchoo
coredata.dc:description: The hydrogen absorption–desorption characteristics of Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) (X =0, 0.04, 0.08, 0.10, 0.12) bcc alloys have been investigated at 303K. The compositions of alloys were chosen for alloys to have equal lattice parameters of about 3.03Å by a linear combination of each element. The alloys of about 20g each were arc-melted into button shaped ingots under argon atmosphere and re-melted five times to improve their homogeneities. The XRD analysis showed that all of the samples prepared had bcc single phases and their lattice parameters were nearly the same regardless of their chemical compositions. The PC isotherms of the alloys revealed that the hydrogen storage capacities of the alloys exceeded 3mass% owing to the control of the lattice parameters and only the second plateau was reversible at room temperature. A reversible structural transition from fcc to bcc occurred with dehydrogenation of the second plateau. Some hydrogen remained unreleased even at a considerable low pressure corresponds to the bcc hydride and needed thermal activation energy for desorption. The hydrogen absorption rate of the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy was considerably fast and more than 95% of the hydrogen was absorbed within the first 10min.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen storage alloy
bcc alloy
TiCrV alloy
Structural transition
PC isotherm

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns of Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) (X =0, 0.04, 0.08, 0.10, 0.12) alloys.
PC isotherms of the Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) alloys.
X-ray diffraction patterns of the Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) alloys obtained after the PC isotherm measurements.
X-ray diffraction patterns of Ti0.24Cr0.36V0.4 alloy with hydrogenation.
DSC patterns of the Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) alloys obtained after the PC isotherm measurements.
Variation of the X-ray diffraction patterns of the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy with dehydrogenation treatments.
Hydrogen absorption curve of the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy while the introduced hydrogen pressure reached the equilibrium pressure, 0.9MPa.
SEM micrograph and EDS mapping of the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy.

article.ce:title: Hydrogen absorption–desorption characteristics of Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) (0≤ X ≤0.12) alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Minerals and Materials Processing Research Division, Korea Institute of Geoscience and Mineral Resources, Gajeong-dong 30, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-350, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Chonnam National University, Yongbong-Dong 300, Buk-gu, Gwangju 500-757, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Advanced Materials Engineering, Hanlyo University, Dokrery 199-4, Kwangyang, Chonnam 545-704, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hydrogen absorption–desorption characteristics of Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) (X =0, 0.04, 0.08, 0.10, 0.12) bcc alloys have been investigated at 303K. The compositions of alloys were chosen for alloys to have equal lattice parameters of about 3.03Å by a linear combination of each element. The alloys of about 20g each were arc-melted into button shaped ingots under argon atmosphere and re-melted five times to improve their homogeneities. The XRD analysis showed that all of the samples prepared had bcc single phases and their lattice parameters were nearly the same regardless of their chemical compositions. The PC isotherms of the alloys revealed that the hydrogen storage capacities of the alloys exceeded 3mass% owing to the control of the lattice parameters and only the second plateau was reversible at room temperature. A reversible structural transition from fcc to bcc occurred with dehydrogenation of the second plateau. Some hydrogen remained unreleased even at a considerable low pressure corresponds to the bcc hydride and needed thermal activation energy for desorption. The hydrogen absorption rate of the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy was considerably fast and more than 95% of the hydrogen was absorbed within the first 10min.

ce:section.ce:para: In commercializing a hydrogen storage material, there are several important factors to be considered: its reaction characteristics with hydrogen such as the temperature and the rate, its cycling characteristics, and its capacity for hydrogen storage, etc.

Some bcc solid solution alloys [1–6] are expected as highly promising hydrogen storage materials due to not only their relatively high storage capacity but also their capability to absorb and desorb hydrogen under ambient conditions. To be practical, however, many of them react slowly with hydrogen. Also, only about 60% of stored hydrogen behaves reversibly because the first plateau pressure is too low to desorb hydrogen even at a considerable low pressure.

It is of fundamental importance to know the structural transitions associated with hydrogenation and dehydrogenation not only to understand the reason why a part of hydrogen is not desorbed even at low pressure but also to foresee the cycling characteristics. One of the present authors has reported on the hydrogen storage characteristics of TiCrV alloys [7] and the structural transitions associated with hydrogenation [8].

In this study, the hydrogen absorption rate and the structural transitions upon dehydrogenation as well as the hydrogen storage capacities of the Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) (0≤ X ≤0.12) alloys were investigated at 303K.

The Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) (X =0, 0.04, 0.08, 0.10, 0.12) alloys were prepared into button shaped ingots of about 20g by arc-melting in a pure argon atmosphere. To prevent segregation of the metal elements, the ingots were turned over and re-melted five times. The ingots were mechanically crushed into powder in air without heat treatment.

The PC isotherms of the samples were measured using powders of 100–300μm size at 303K up to a hydrogen pressure of 5MPa using a volumetric method. The crystal structures and lattice parameters were studied using X-ray diffraction data obtained by Cu Kα radiation. The sample powders smaller than 32μm were used for the XRD analysis. After the PC isotherm measurements, a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) analysis was carried out to observe the temperature range where the leftover hydrogen was desorbed. The hydrogen absorption curves of the samples were plotted using the data obtained from the real time measurement of the decrease in the hydrogen pressure as hydrogen is absorbed by the samples. Using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) and an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS), the morphology and the compositions of the samples were investigated.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the sample powders, all exhibiting the characteristics of the bcc structure. Moreover, it is notable that their lattice parameters are almost identical regardless of the X values varying between 0 and 0.12 as illustrated by the straight line through the strongest peaks in the patterns, and this is to be expected from the previous work [7].

Fig. 2 shows the PC isotherms measured at 303K. The hydrogen storage capacities of the tested alloys exceeded 3mass% except in the case of the Ti0.22Cr0.28V0.5 alloy. The reversible hydrogen storage capacities were also relatively large. This is due to the controlled lattice parameters in the range of 3.02–3.04Å, which was mentioned in the previous works [7,9]. The reason why the storage capacity of the Ti0.22Cr0.28V0.5 alloy was smaller than those of the other alloys will be explained below.

In addition to the reversible hydrogen storage capacity, the cyclic capability induced by pressure variation is also an important factor to be considered to develop a practical hydrogen storage material. To investigate this capability as well as the reason why a part of hydrogen is not desorbed even at low pressure, the XRD patterns of the samples that had already undergone the PC isotherm measurements were analyzed. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , unlike others, the Ti0.22Cr0.28V0.5 alloy exhibits an extra peak set of a bcc phase with a smaller lattice parameter, which is denoted as black square marks. It turns out that this extra bcc phase is the unreacted part of the sample after the hydrogen absorption reaction. This indicates the incompleteness of the activation treatment and explains the smaller hydrogen storage capacity of the Ti0.22Cr0.28V0.5 alloy.

Fig. 3 also shows that in addition to the bcc phase there are small peaks indicating the presence of the fcc phase in the samples. This result can be understood well by considering the structural transition with hydrogenation. Fig. 4 clearly shows the gradual structural changes of the Ti0.24Cr0.36V0.4 alloy with the progress of the hydrogenation reaction. As hydrogenation progresses, the bcc phase expands first and then the structural transition from bcc to fcc occurs [8]. Therefore, it can be said from Figs. 3 and 4 that a reversible structural transition from fcc to bcc occurs with dehydrogenation.

As mentioned previously, the DSC analyses of the samples were carried out to observe the temperature range in which the leftover hydrogen after the PC isotherm measurements could be desorbed. The results are shown in Fig. 5 . Regardless of the compositions, in general there are one clear exothermic peak and two somewhat obscure endothermic peaks. The latter appears to be caused by the desorption of hydrogen, while the former is due to the oxidation of the samples during the measurement. From this, one can note that hydrogen desorption occurs in two temperature regions, that is, the lower and the higher ones. It is also noteworthy that the endothermic peak of the high temperature region becomes deep as the titanium content increases. It corresponds to the fact that in the ternary TiCrV system titanium binds with hydrogen most strongly.

To investigate the structural changes during the DSC measurement, XRD analysis was conducted after each treatment step, using the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy as samples, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . The patterns s4 and s5 represent the results obtained at 360 and 600°C, respectively. Comparing the pattern s3 with those of s2 and s1, one can see that the hydrogen left in the fcc phase after PC isotherm measurement can be desorbed simply by evacuating the sample for 1h. Also, it can be seen that the pattern s4 is nearly the same with s3, which means that the endothermic peak of the low temperature region in Fig. 5 corresponds to the hydrogen desorption of the fcc phase.

The pattern s5 indicates that the hydrogen in the bcc phase of s4 was desorbed by heating the sample to 600°C, resulting in the decrease in the lattice parameter of the phase. Combining this result with that of Fig. 5, one can see that the endothermic peak of the high temperature region indicates the hydrogen desorption of the bcc phase. It should also be noted that the lattice parameter of the s5 became almost the same with that of s1.

In summary, the hydrogen desorption in the low temperature region corresponds to that of the fcc phase, which can be achieved by evacuation only for the sample, while the hydrogen desorption in the high temperature region corresponds to that of the bcc phase, which needs the thermal activation energy.

Fig. 7 shows the hydrogen absorption curve of the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy. The hydrogen pressure decreases until an equilibrium pressure is reached, resulting in the hydrogen absorption of about 3mass%. Note that the hydrogen absorption rate of the alloy is considerably fast and that more than 95% of the hydrogen is absorbed within the first 10min.

Fig. 8 shows the SEM micrograph and the results of the EDS mapping for the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy. No second phase or macro-segregation was found in the micrograph. From the results of the EDS mapping, however, it can be seen that the micro-segregation exists, indicating the need of the heat treatment.

The hydrogen absorption–desorption characteristics of Ti(0.22+ X)Cr(0.28+1.5X)V(0.5−2.5X) (0≤ X ≤0.12) alloys have been investigated at 303K. The conclusions of this study are as follows:
1.
All of the samples prepared had bcc structures and their lattice parameters were nearly the same regardless of their chemical compositions.
2.
Controlling the lattice parameters within a certain limit (3.02–3.04Å), the hydrogen storage capacities of the alloys exceeded 3mass%. The reversible hydrogen storage capacities were also relatively large.
3.
The reversible structural transition from fcc to bcc occurs with dehydrogenation. Comparing this result with the previous one [8] that showed the structural transition from bcc to fcc upon hydrogenation, it suggests the cyclic capability induced by hydrogen pressure variation.
4.
The hydrogen unreleased even at a considerable low pressure corresponds to the bcc hydride and needs the thermal activation energy for desorption.
5.
The hydrogen absorption rate of the Ti0.32Cr0.43V0.25 alloy was considerably fast and more than 95% of the hydrogen was absorbed within the first 10min.
6.
Micro-segregation was observed, while no second phase or macro-segregation was found.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was conducted as a part of the 21st Century Frontier R&D Program for Hydrogen Energy with funding of the Ministry of Science and Technology of Korea.
